structure.notes: The note preserves the partial-identification locked tuple, includes the required Stage 0 sections, labels assumptions, distinguishes the bounding functional from the target, and gives an actionable math-objects checklist with a substrate bypass record.

novelty.notes: The derived kernel is a standard support-function reduction plus an elementary positive-rank-one finite-matrix calibration condition; it does not meet the enforced flagship floor because it opens no new partial-identification regime, proves no named-estimator equivalence/non-equivalence frontier, and strictly extends no cited result class.

correctness.notes: The support-function identities, calibration-minor equivalence, ranking-witness algebra, and four-atom D1 calculation are mathematically coherent under the note's abstract local support-set assumptions.

perItemFindings.label: Theorem 1 / Lemma 1
perItemFindings.one_line: The sharp bounds are direct evaluations of support functions of L2, Linfty mean-zero, and covariate-only L2 balls, not a new partial-identification regime.

perItemFindings.label: Theorem 2
perItemFindings.one_line: The scalar-calibration frontier is the positive rank-one characterization of a finite penalty matrix, an elementary linear-algebra diagnostic below flagship tier.

perItemFindings.label: Proposition 2 / Conjecture 2 witness
perItemFindings.one_line: The negative result refutes an internal open-set wording rather than a specific published estimator or workflow, and the corrected witness is a finite-support calibration residual rather than a generic-class obstruction.

verbatim_critique: The artifact is structurally reviewable and the main algebra is correct under the declared local support-function abstraction. The problem is novelty relative to the orchestrator-enforced flagship target. Theorem \ref{thm:support-bounds} evaluates three classical support functions: Cauchy--Schwarz for the L2 ball, the standard quotient-dual identity for the mean-zero Linfty ball, and the tower-property projection for covariate-only tilts. Theorem \ref{thm:calibration-frontier} then reduces all-pairs scalar calibration to vanishing 2x2 minors of the finite matrix Q_{Pi_*}, equivalently positive rank one. That is useful bookkeeping, but it is not a flagship partial-identification theorem: it does not derive a new sharp bound class beyond the stipulated local norm balls, does not prove an if-and-only-if frontier between two named published estimators or identification strategies, and does not strictly extend a cited open regime. The Conjecture 2 discussion is also not flagship-grade negative-result novelty: it corrects the internal claim that exact equality of two CH norms holds on an open set, but it does not name a published target that relies on that exact-equality shortcut, and the surviving witness is a single finite-support example plus an open non-calibration residual. Therefore the current derivation is below the required novelty_target=flagship even though its structure and correctness dimensions pass.
